faq.question: What is Diwani calligraphy?
faq.answer: Diwani (خط ديواني) is the elegant cursive script developed in the 16th century Ottoman Empire as the official script of the Sultan's chancery — the Diwan — from which it takes its name. Classical hand-drawn Diwani is characterized by tightly interlocking cursive letters, flowing curves, decorative overlaps, and a slight upward slant. It was designed to be simultaneously beautiful and difficult to forge, making it perfect for imperial decrees and diplomatic correspondence.

faq.question: What is Jali Diwani?
faq.answer: Jali Diwani (جلي ديواني) is the most ornate variant of Diwani calligraphy, developed shortly after standard Diwani. In Jali Diwani, calligraphers filled the negative space between letters with a dense network of dots and small decorative strokes, producing an extraordinarily intricate woven pattern. Jali Diwani was used exclusively for the Sultan's Tughra (imperial signature) and the most important state documents. Because of its extreme complexity, no free digital font faithfully reproduces Jali Diwani — commercial specialty fonts or hand-drawn calligraphy are required.

faq.question: What does Diwani mean?
faq.answer: The name Diwani comes from the Arabic word Diwan (ديوان), meaning the Sultan's chancery, council, or state office in the Ottoman Empire. The script was named after this administrative body because it was the official script used for chancery documents — imperial decrees, diplomatic correspondence, and state records. The name captures the script's origin as an administrative rather than religious calligraphy tradition.

faq.question: What is the difference between Diwani and Thuluth?
faq.answer: Thuluth is the classical religious script used in mosque inscriptions and Qur'anic chapter headings — tall, ornate, and reverent. Diwani is the Ottoman administrative script — cursive, tight, and originally secular. Thuluth carries mosque and religious gravity; Diwani carries imperial court and ceremonial gravity. Both are ornate and formal, but they emerged in different institutional traditions (mosque vs. court) with different visual signatures.

faq.question: Which font renders Diwani calligraphy on a computer?
faq.answer: The best freely available digital font for Diwani-style rendering is Aref Ruqaa, designed by Abdo Beshkoo in a graceful cursive style rooted in the broader Ottoman calligraphy tradition (Ruqʿah and Diwani influences). It supports full Arabic Unicode with proper ligatures and harakat, and is used by the generator on this page. For truly ornate hand-drawn classical Diwani (or the intricate Jali Diwani), a commercial specialty font or a professional calligrapher would be required — but Aref Ruqaa is the accessible high-quality free standard.

content: Naskh calligraphy (خط النسخ, khaṭṭ al-naskh) is the standardized cursive Arabic script that has served as the primary calligraphy of Arabic writing since the 10th century CE. Its name comes from the Arabic word naskh (نسخ) meaning "to copy" or "to transcribe" — reflecting its origin as the script used by scribes to produce copies of the Qur'an and religious texts. Today, Naskh is the script of virtually every modern printed Qur'an worldwide, and its digital descendants (Amiri, Noto Naskh Arabic, KFGQPC Uthman Taha Naskh) are used in newspapers, books, websites, and street signs across the Arabic-reading world.

Naskh is designed for one purpose above all: maximum readability. Its letters are compact, well-proportioned, cursive but restrained, and use consistent stroke weights that render clearly at both small text size and larger display use. Where Thuluth is decorative and ornate, and Kufic is bold and geometric, Naskh is the utilitarian workhorse — the script you actually read.

The tool on this page uses Amiri — the most respected free digital Naskh font, designed by Khaled Hosny in the classical Naskh tradition. Amiri faithfully renders the Naskh style used in traditional Qur'anic manuscripts and printed Qur'ans, with full support for Arabic ligatures, harakat, and proper contextual letter shaping. For most Arabic reading and writing today, if the script is not visibly Thuluth, Kufic, or Diwani, it is almost certainly Naskh or a close descendant.

heroImage.alt: The name Allah (الله) rendered in classical Naskh Arabic calligraphy (Amiri) in gold on deep navy background

content: The Naskh script is defined by five characteristics that together produce its unmatched readability:

1. Compact, well-proportioned letters — Naskh letters are neither exaggerated tall like Thuluth nor blocky wide like Kufic. Each letter fills a comfortable middle proportion designed for reading in extended prose. Vertical letters (alif, lam, ta) are moderately tall; curved letters (nun, ya, ra) sweep naturally without excess flourish.
2. Even, restrained stroke weight — Naskh uses a nearly uniform stroke weight throughout, with only subtle thick-thin variation at letter joins. This produces text that reads smoothly at any size without the visual noise of dramatic weight contrast.
3. Full harakat support — Naskh naturally supports the complete set of Arabic vowel and diacritic marks (fatha, kasra, damma, shadda, sukun), which is why it became the definitive Qur'anic script. Every subtle pronunciation cue can be encoded above or below the letters.
4. Clean cursive joining — Naskh letters connect through simple, hygienic joins that maintain letter individuality. Where Diwani interlocks decoratively and Thuluth flows ornately, Naskh joins are functional and clear.
5. Grid-friendly baseline — Naskh sits on a stable baseline with predictable letter heights, making it the only Arabic script that reliably works for justified columns of text — one reason it dominated Arabic printing from the 15th century onward.

These qualities make Naskh the standard script for reading Arabic. If you have ever seen printed Arabic — in a Qur'an, newspaper, book, website, or road sign — you have almost certainly been reading Naskh or a modernized descendant of it.

content: Three free digital Naskh fonts dominate modern Arabic typography — each with a different focus:

1. Amiri — designed by Khaled Hosny, Amiri is a classical revival of the elegant Naskh style used in the Bulaq Press (Egyptian government printing office, 19th century) editions of Arabic literary and religious texts. Amiri is the closest free font to traditional manuscript Naskh and is what the generator on this page uses. Available free from Google Fonts.

2. Noto Naskh Arabic — designed by Google as part of the Noto ("no tofu") font family, Noto Naskh Arabic is a clean, screen-optimized Naskh with excellent multi-language support. It is the default Arabic font in Android, ChromeOS, and many web contexts. Best choice for websites, apps, and any digital interface where consistent cross-platform rendering matters. Available free from Google Fonts.

3. KFGQPC Uthman Taha Naskh — the official Naskh font of the King Fahd Glorious Qur'an Printing Complex (KFGQPC) in Medina, Saudi Arabia. This is the font printed on the standard Mushaf al-Madinah Qur'an distributed worldwide since 1985 — the most widely printed Qur'an edition in history. Designed by the calligrapher Uthman Taha, it is considered the definitive modern Qur'anic Naskh. Available free from the KFGQPC website; used by countless Qur'an apps and websites.
To download your Naskh calligraphy design from the generator above:

PNG (Transparent) — best for overlaying Naskh text on backgrounds, prayer cards, and educational materials.
PNG (White or Black) — great for printed materials, community bulletins, wall art, and framed prints.
SVG — best for large prints and posters that need scaling.
JPG — smallest file size for casual sharing.
All Naskh calligraphy downloads are free — no watermark, no sign-up, no account required.

The relationship between Naskh and the Qur'an is one of the deepest in Islamic history. From the 11th century onward, Naskh gradually replaced the earlier Kufic script as the standard calligraphy for Qur'anic manuscripts. The transition was driven by Naskh's superior readability — as Qur'an memorization expanded across the growing Muslim world, a script that could be read fluently by non-experts became essential.

By the 16th–19th century, Naskh had become the almost universal script for handwritten Qur'ans in Ottoman, Persian, Central Asian, and Arab manuscript traditions. Master calligraphers spent years training in classical Naskh — most famously Sheikh Hamdullah (Ottoman, d. 1520), whose Naskh established the modern proportional system, and Hafiz Osman (d. 1698), whose 25+ hand-copied Qur'ans set the standard for later generations.

In the modern printed era, the standard-setting event was the 1985 publication of the Mushaf al-Madinah — the printed Qur'an produced by the King Fahd Glorious Qur'an Printing Complex (KFGQPC) in Medina, Saudi Arabia. The KFGQPC hired the Syrian calligrapher Uthman Taha to hand-write the entire Qur'an in a modern Naskh style, and this handwritten master was then photographed and typeset. Since then, the KFGQPC Uthman Taha Naskh has printed over 300 million copies of the Qur'an distributed to mosques worldwide — making it, by circulation, the most widely reproduced calligraphy in human history.

Today, when you open a Qur'an — printed, digital, or handwritten — you are almost certainly reading Naskh. The other Islamic scripts (Thuluth for chapter headings, Diwani for ornament, Kufic for architecture) surround the Qur'anic text, but the sacred text itself is Naskh.

sections.h2: Naskh vs Thuluth vs Diwani vs Kufic
content: Naskh is one of the six classical Arabic scripts. Its distinguishing feature is that it is the only one designed primarily for reading rather than decoration. Here's how it compares to the three most common ornamental alternatives:

Naskh vs Thuluth: Thuluth is the ornate ceremonial script used in mosque inscriptions and Qur'anic chapter headings — tall, elegant, and decorative. Naskh is the utilitarian body-text script used for the Qur'anic verses themselves. Every printed Qur'an typically pairs the two: Thuluth chapter titles surrounding Naskh main text. See our Thuluth Calligraphy page for more.

Naskh vs Diwani: Diwani is the ornate Ottoman court cursive, developed for imperial documents and diplomatic correspondence. Naskh is the everyday script — designed for wide reading rather than ceremonial elegance. Diwani says "imperial ornament"; Naskh says "clear communication." See our Diwani Calligraphy page.

Naskh vs Kufic: Kufic is the oldest Arabic script — angular, geometric, monumental. Naskh replaced Kufic as the standard Qur'anic script in the 11th century precisely because it is easier to read. Kufic remains preferred for architectural inscription and modern logo design; Naskh dominates everywhere Arabic must be read fluently. See our Kufic Calligraphy page.

When to choose Naskh: reading and studying Qur'an, Islamic educational materials, printed and digital Arabic text, community mosque bulletins, children's Arabic learning resources, and any project where legibility matters more than decoration.
